rigidity_reasoning: Butterfly is a phase in the life cycle of a lepidopteran insect. An individual starts as a larva (caterpillar) and becomes a butterfly; since it can exist without being a butterfly, the property is not essential to the individual.
identity_reasoning: As a biological organism, a Butterfly carries an identity condition based on biological continuity and genetic uniqueness, allowing individual instances to be distinguished from one another and tracked through time.
own_identity_reasoning: Butterfly is a natural kind (a species) that defines its own global identity conditions for its instances, rather than inheriting them from a more general substance-sortal like a parent category that manages identity for disparate kinds.
unity_reasoning: A butterfly is an organic, biological whole whose parts (wings, thorax, antennae) are functionally integrated and depend on their relationship to the whole to exist as a single, discrete individual.
dependence_reasoning: A butterfly is an independent biological organism. While it relies on an ecosystem and resources to survive, its existence is not defined by an intrinsic ontological dependence on a specific, separate entity in the way a role like 'Parasite' or 'Child' is defined by its relationship to another.

rigidity_reasoning: The property 'Red' is non-rigid because an entity (like a car) can be red at one point in time and painted another color later without losing its identity. While some entities might be red in all possible worlds, others gain or lose the property contingently.
identity_reasoning: Red is a property (quality) that does not provide a principled way to distinguish one instance of the color from another or re-identify it over time independently of a substrate.
own_identity_reasoning: Since Red is -I (it does not carry an identity condition), it cannot supply one (+O requires +I). As a property or quality, Red relies on the identity of the object it characterizes.
unity_reasoning: The property 'Red' refers to a color quality rather than an integrated whole. Its extension consists of disparate objects (apples, cars, sensors) that do not form a single functional or structural unit.
dependence_reasoning: Red is a property or quality (specifically a color); it cannot exist independently and must always be manifested by or inhered in a physical object or substrate.

rigidity_reasoning: Food is a role played by physical substances. An entity (like an apple) can be food at one point but ceases to be food once it is fully digested or rotted, yet it continues to exist as matter. Being food is not essential to the existence of the substance.
identity_reasoning: Food is a role or a mass noun; as a role, it does not provide its own identity conditions but relies on those of the underlying physical objects, and as a mass substance, it lacks a principled way to distinguish or re-identify discrete instances over time.
own_identity_reasoning: Since 'Food' was determined to be -I (not carrying a consistent identity condition), it cannot supply its own identity condition (+O). It is a functional role played by various entities (plants, animals, processed products) that inherit their identity from their respective substance types.
unity_reasoning: Food is a functional classification based on consumption rather than a structural property; its instances range from discrete integrated wholes (an apple) to non-unified substances (a bowl of soup) or collections of materials.
dependence_reasoning: Food is an extrinsic, relational role. An entity only becomes 'Food' in relation to a Consumer (organism) that can derive nourishment from it. Without the existence of a potential consumer, the entity is merely matter (such as a plant or animal) but not food.

rigidity_reasoning: Being an animal is an essential property of its instances. An entity that is an animal cannot cease to be an animal while continuing to exist; it is a fundamental taxonomic category that defines the identity of the object across its lifespan.
identity_reasoning: As a biological organism, an Animal carries an identity condition provided by its specific genetic makeup and spatio-temporal continuity, allowing for the distinction and re-identification of individuals.
own_identity_reasoning: Animal is a base sortal that supplies its own global identity condition to its instances, rather than inheriting its primary identity from a more general supertype.
unity_reasoning: Every animal is a biological organism that functions as an integrated whole. It possesses a mereological structure where parts (organs, cells) are functionally interdependent, establishing a clear boundary and unity.
dependence_reasoning: An Animal is an independent entity that does not intrinsically require the existence of another specific entity to be defined as what it is, unlike roles such as 'Predator' or 'Pet' which depend on a prey or an owner.

rigidity_reasoning: While an object might be a 'RedApple' now, it can change color (e.g., through ripening or chemical alteration) and remain the same individual fruit. Therefore, being red is not essential to the identity of every instance of this class.
identity_reasoning: RedApple is a specialization of a physical object (Apple), which carries a specific identity condition based on spatio-temporal continuity and biological origin, allowing individual instances to be distinguished and re-identified over time.
own_identity_reasoning: RedApple does not supply a new identity condition; it inherits its identity condition from the more general rigid property Apple.
unity_reasoning: A RedApple represents a biological organism (or part of one) that spans a self-contained spatial region with a clear boundary, where the parts (peel, flesh, core) are functionally and structurally integrated into a single whole.
dependence_reasoning: While RedApple implies a specific quality (color), an instance of a red apple does not necessarily depend on another distinct entity to exist in the way a 'Student' depends on a 'School'. The apple is an independent physical object.

rigidity_reasoning: A group is defined by its existence as a collective entity; an instance of a group cannot cease to be a group and still exist. While its membership may change, the property of being a 'group' is essential to the identity of the entity itself across all possible worlds.
identity_reasoning: A Group carries an identity condition, typically defined by its membership or a specific founding charter/context, allowing one group to be distinguished from another.
own_identity_reasoning: A Group supplies its own identity condition that is distinct from its individual members or other types of collections. The identity of a group is typically based on its specific composition and structure at a given time (extensional) or its foundational mission/charter (intensional), which is not inherited from a more general supertype.
unity_reasoning: A Group is an aggregate or collection of entities whose identity is defined by the sum of its members rather than by fixed, intrinsic functional or biological integration that constitutes a single whole.
dependence_reasoning: A group is an ontological entity that necessarily depends on the existence of its constituent members; a group cannot exist without the entities that comprise it.

rigidity_reasoning: A Country is considered a rigid entity because a specific instance that is a Country is essentially a Country; it cannot stop being a Country and become a different kind of entity (like a person or a physical stone) while retaining its identity.
identity_reasoning: Country carries an identity condition as instances can be uniquely identified and tracked over time through criteria such as territorial boundaries, ISO codes, and legal recognition, allowing for re-identification despite changes in government or population.
own_identity_reasoning: The concept 'Country' supplies its own global identity condition (typically based on distinct geographic sovereignty and political recognition) rather than inheriting it from a more general class like 'Geographic Region' or 'Organization'.
unity_reasoning: A Country is an integrated whole defined by clear geographical boundaries, a unified legal/political system, and a functional structure that binds its constituent parts (provinces, citizens, institutions) into a single entity.
dependence_reasoning: A Country is an ontologically dependent entity because its existence depends on other entities such as a territory, a population, and a governing system (sovereign state).

rigidity_reasoning: A particular amount of matter cannot cease to be an amount of matter without ceasing to exist. It is a rigid property because being a quantity of matter is essential to the identity of the entity itself.
identity_reasoning: AmountOfMatter is typically considered a non-sortal concept that does not provide its own criteria for distinguishing one instance from another; identity is usually provided by the object the matter constitutes, rather than the matter itself.
own_identity_reasoning: Since the entity is analyzed as -I (does not carry an identity condition), it cannot logically supply its own identity condition (+O). It lacks a mechanism to distinguish specific instances of matter throughout their existence.
unity_reasoning: AmountOfMatter represents a mass noun/quantity. Any sub-part of an amount of matter is itself an amount of matter, and arbitrary parts can be combined without creating a naturally bounded or integrated functional whole.
dependence_reasoning: An amount of matter does not require the existence of any other specific entity or instance to exist; it is defined by its own composition and extension rather than its relationship to an external host or system.

rigidity_reasoning: PhysicalObject is a rigid property because if an entity is a physical object, it cannot cease to be a physical object without losing its identity (i.e., ceasing to exist). It is a fundamental property that is essential to all its instances across all possible worlds.
identity_reasoning: PhysicalObject carries an identity condition typically based on spatio-temporal continuity and topological integrity, allowing instances to be uniquely identified and tracked throughout their lifecycle.
own_identity_reasoning: A PhysicalObject is a sortal that provides its own criterion for individuation and identity (e.g., spatio-temporal continuity), rather than inheriting it from a more general category.
unity_reasoning: The term PhysicalObject is a high-level generalization that includes both integrated wholes (like a stone) and non-integrated aggregates (like a pile of sand). Since it does not carry a specific unity criterion that applies to all its instances, it is non-unifying.
dependence_reasoning: A PhysicalObject is a fundamental entity that does not inherently require the existence of another specific entity to exist. While it may depend on space or time, it is not definitionally dependent on a specific relationship with another category of object in the way a 'Student' depends on a 'Teacher' or 'School'.

rigidity_reasoning: Being a group of people is an essential property of the entity; if an entity ceases to be a group of people, it ceases to exist as that specific entity. It is not a contingent role that an entity can take on and later discard while maintaining its ontological identity.
identity_reasoning: A Group of People carries an identity condition, typically defined by its membership composition, its founding criteria, or its formal legal status, allowing it to be distinguished from other groups and tracked over time.
own_identity_reasoning: A GroupOfPeople supplies its own identity condition based on the specific collection or membership criteria of individuals, rather than inheriting its principle of identity from a more general supertype like 'Object'.
unity_reasoning: A GroupOfPeople is an aggregate whose boundaries are defined by its members rather than a formal integrated structure that makes it an intrinsic whole. It lacks the internal functional integrity required for +U.
dependence_reasoning: A group is ontologically dependent on its members; if the individuals comprising the group cease to exist, the group itself can no longer exist.

rigidity_reasoning: Being a living being is a fundamental ontological property; an entity that is categorized as a living being cannot cease to be one without ceasing to exist as that specific entity. It is essential to all its instances in any possible world.
identity_reasoning: A LivingBeing carries an identity condition through its biological and structural continuity, allowing for the distinction and re-identification of specific instances throughout their life cycle.
own_identity_reasoning: A LivingBeing supplies its own identity condition (IC) globally for its instances, determining the necessary criteria for individuality and persistence that are not dependent on a more general supertype.
unity_reasoning: A living being is an integrated biological system where its components (organs, cells, etc.) function together to maintain the whole. It possesses a clear mereological structure and internal synergy that defines it as a single unity rather than an arbitrary collection.
dependence_reasoning: A living being does not intrinsically depend on the existence of a specific other entity to be defined or to exist in the same way a 'Student' depends on a 'School'. While it requires a biological environment, it is not ontologically dependent on another specific individual or institution for its identity.

rigidity_reasoning: SocialEntity is a rigid property because an entity that is categorized as a social entity (such as an organization or a legal person) cannot cease to be a social entity without losing its identity. It is an essential characteristic of the entity in all possible worlds.
identity_reasoning: Social entities (e.g., organizations, states, clubs) are generally identifiable and re-identifiable over time through specific constitutive rules, charters, or legal identifiers, even if their constituent members change.
own_identity_reasoning: SocialEntity supplies its own unique identity condition (such as legal registration, charter, or defined social membership) that distinguishes it from other types of entities at the same level of abstraction.
unity_reasoning: SocialEntity is a broad category encompassing both unified wholes (e.g., an Organization or a Person) and non-unified or loosely bounded collectives (e.g., a Social Class or a Demographic). Because the property does not consistently provide a single, uniform criterion for identifying the boundaries of its instances, it lacks a common internal unity.
dependence_reasoning: Social entities are dependent entities because their existence necessarily relies on the existence of underlying physical or biological entities (specifically persons or agents) to constitute them.

rigidity_reasoning: A Location is a rigid entity because its identity is defined by its coordinates or spatial boundaries; an entity that is a Location cannot cease to be a Location without ceasing to exist as that specific spatial entity.
identity_reasoning: Location carries an identity condition because it can be uniquely identified and distinguished through spatial coordinates or reference systems, allowing for re-identification of the same point or region in a frame of reference.
own_identity_reasoning: A Location supplies its own identity condition based on spatial coordinates or a fixed topological reference. It does not look to a more general class to determine the criteria for being the 'same' location.
unity_reasoning: A location is typically defined by coordinates or spatial extents that lack intrinsic boundaries or a functional/biological integration of parts. Since locations are often arbitrary subsets of space, they do not necessarily form a unified whole.
dependence_reasoning: A location is an extrinsic property of an entity that identifies its position within a spatial frame of reference; it cannot exist without a space or an entity to define its coordinates.

rigidity_reasoning: Being a fruit is an essential property of an organism's reproductive structure. An instance of a fruit cannot cease to be a fruit without losing its identity or becoming a different entity entirely.
identity_reasoning: Fruit identifies a distinct physical object with spatio-temporal continuity. It carries identity conditions (such as physical boundaries, genetic markers, and origin) that allow individuals to be distinguished and re-identified.
own_identity_reasoning: Fruit supplies its own identity conditions as a rigid sortal that defines the criteria for being a specific biological unit, rather than inheriting identity from a more general substance or role.
unity_reasoning: A Fruit is an organic whole with a specific biological structure (pericarp, seeds, skin) and clear boundaries. Its parts are functionally integrated to serve a specific botanical purpose, making each instance an intrinsic unit.
dependence_reasoning: A fruit is inherently dependent on a parent plant (organism) to come into existence and fulfill its ontological definition as a reproductive organ or botanical product.

rigidity_reasoning: Being a vertebrate is an essential biological property of an organism; an entity that is a vertebrate cannot cease to be one without losing its identity or ceasing to exist as that entity.
identity_reasoning: As a specialization of 'Animal', 'Vertebrate' carries an identity condition based on biological and spatio-temporal continuity, allowing for the re-identification of specific individual instances over time.
own_identity_reasoning: As a biological taxon, Vertebrate supplies its own specific identity condition based on physiological and genetic commonalities (presence of a backbone/spinal column) that distinguish members as distinct individuals throughout their existence.
unity_reasoning: Vertebrates are biological organisms that function as integrated, self-organizing wholes with a defined musculoskeletal structure (a backbone) that provides a clear principle of unity for their constituent parts.
dependence_reasoning: A vertebrate is an intrinsic biological categorization. Its existence as a biological organism does not necessarily depend on a relationship with a specific external entity or institution to maintain its identity, unlike roles such as 'Student' or 'Spouse'.

rigidity_reasoning: Caterpillar is a phase in the lifecycle of an insect. An individual organism that is currently a caterpillar will lose this property when it undergoes metamorphosis to become a butterfly or moth, but it remains the same identity.
identity_reasoning: As a biological organism, Caterpillar carries an identity condition through its unique spatio-temporal path and genetic signature, allowing a specific individual to be identified and tracked even as it undergoes physical growth.
own_identity_reasoning: Caterpillar represents a biological kind that supplies its own identity condition as a organismic individual; it is not a property inherited from a more general substance sortal, as the identity of being a caterpillar is intrinsic to its specific developmental stage and biological nature.
unity_reasoning: A caterpillar is a biological organism with a functionally integrated anatomy and a clear boundary that defines it as a discrete whole rather than a mere aggregate.
dependence_reasoning: A Caterpillar is an independent physical entity. While it requires biological processes to survive, its existence is not defined by a relationship to another specific external entity in the way a 'Parasite' or 'Child' is.

rigidity_reasoning: A person cannot cease to be a person without ceasing to exist. It is a fundamental property that remains true for an instance in all possible worlds where it exists.
identity_reasoning: Person is a sortal property that carries identity conditions, allowing instances to be uniquely identified and tracked through time using criteria such as DNA, fingerprints, or socio-legal identifiers.
own_identity_reasoning: A Person is a functional type that introduces its own unique criteria for identity (such as biological or legal markers) rather than inheriting them from a more general super-property.
unity_reasoning: A Person is an intrinsic biological and functional whole characterized by a specific structure and interdependence of parts. It possesses clear boundaries and internal synergy, distinguishing it from an arbitrary collection or fragment.
dependence_reasoning: A person is a rigid entity that does not require the existence of another specific entity (other than itself) to maintain its identity or existence as a person.

rigidity_reasoning: LegalAgent is anti-rigid because it is a role entity. An individual (such as a person or a corporation) becomes a LegalAgent through a specific legal status or power of attorney and can cease being a LegalAgent without losing its core identity.
identity_reasoning: LegalAgent carries an identity condition because its instances (such as persons or corporations) must be uniquely identifiable and re-identifiable over time to be subjects of rights and obligations, typically through legal identifiers or registration.
own_identity_reasoning: LegalAgent supplies its own identity condition by establishing the criteria for what constitutes a distinct entity capable of holding rights and duties (such as Registration Numbers or unique legal designations), which differentiates it from other generic classes.
unity_reasoning: LegalAgent is a functional role that can be fulfilled by diverse entities such as a single human (a whole) or a corporation (a complex aggregate). Because it does not specify a consistent internal mereological structure for all instances, it lacks a unifying principle.
dependence_reasoning: A Legal Agent is intrinsically dependent on a legal system (normative framework) and a principal entity (individual or organization) that grants authority. Without these external entities, the status of 'Legal Agent' cannot exist.

rigidity_reasoning: Being an 'Entity' is a fundamental ontological necessity; an entity cannot cease to be an entity without also ceasing to exist entirely in any possible world.
identity_reasoning: The term 'Entity' is a root-level property that is too general to carry its own Identity Condition; it subsumes multiple types of entities (persons, physical objects, abstract sets) which have incompatible ways of being identified.
own_identity_reasoning: Since 'Entity' was determined to be -I (not carrying a specific Identity Condition), it cannot supply its own (O). Furthermore, 'Entity' is a top-level category that is too abstract to supply a global identity condition used to distinguish individuals of its type.
unity_reasoning: The term 'Entity' is the root concept in many ontologies and includes both unified wholes (like a Person) and non-unified aggregates or sums (like an Amount of Sand). Since it does not carry a common principle of unity for all its instances, it is non-unifying.
dependence_reasoning: As the most general category, an Entity does not inherently require the existence of any other specific external entity to sustain its own definition or existence.

rigidity_reasoning: Being an Organization is an essential property of its instances. An entity that is an organization cannot stop being an organization without ceasing to exist as that specific entity.
identity_reasoning: Organizations carry an identity condition (IC) often defined by legal registration, charters, or unique tax identifiers, allowing them to be uniquely distinguished and re-identified over time despite changes in membership or location.
own_identity_reasoning: Organization is a rigid property that supplies its own identity condition (often based on legal registration, founding charters, or unique tax identifiers) rather than inheriting its identity from a more general category.
unity_reasoning: An organization is a functional whole composed of integrated members, roles, and resources bound by a common identity or purpose, rather than a mere arbitrary collection of individuals.
dependence_reasoning: An Organization is an external dependent entity because its existence necessarily depends on the existence of its constituent members (e.g., persons or other organizations) and often a founding legal or social framework.

rigidity_reasoning: Acting as an agent is a contingent role. An entity (like a person or software) can gain or lose the property of being an agent while maintaining its underlying identity.
identity_reasoning: Agent carries an identity condition because its instances (such as persons or software systems) are distinct entities that can be re-identified over time through their specific substrate or origin, even if the role of 'Agent' is temporary.
own_identity_reasoning: While Agent is +I because it carries identity, it is typically a role-like or mixin property that characterizes entities that act; it does not supply a unique global identity condition itself, but rather inherits it from more foundational rigid types such as Person or Organization.
unity_reasoning: An Agent is a functional whole that must possess internal integration and a specific boundary to maintain the capacity for action and intentionality; a mere collection of parts cannot act as an agent without being organized as a unified entity.
dependence_reasoning: The concept of an Agent is intrinsically dependent on an Action or a Goal; an entity can only be characterized as an agent in relation to the activities it performs or the principal it represents.

rigidity_reasoning: Being an 'Apple' is essential to the identity of any instance of it. An entity that is an apple cannot cease to be an apple without losing its identity or ceasing to exist as that entity.
identity_reasoning: As a physical object, an apple carries an identity condition based on its unique spatio-temporal continuity and specific physical characteristics, allowing for individual instances to be distinguished and re-identified over time.
own_identity_reasoning: Apple is a rigid sortal that supplies its own identity condition for instances based on their physical and biological boundaries. It does not inherit its identity from a more general class; rather, it provides the criteria to distinguish one apple from another.
unity_reasoning: An Apple is an integrated biological whole with a well-defined boundary and a functional mereological structure where the parts (skin, flesh, seeds, stem) are essential to the unity of the specific entity.
dependence_reasoning: An apple is a physical object that can exist independently. While it requires a tree to be brought into existence, once it exists, its identity and continued existence do not intrinsically depend on the continued presence or existence of a specific separate entity.
